claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, could you tell me how many children you would like to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to claim Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! It's lovely to meet you. Let me ask a few questions about Alex to assess your eligibility.

How old is Alex?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. Since Alex is 17, I need to ask: is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as A-levels, T-levels, NVQs, or a similar course), or has he recently left education?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in any education or training at the moment. He left school and isn't doing any courses.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. I have a couple more questions to clarify Alex's situation.

Is Alex currently registered for work, education, or training with a careers service, Connexions, or similar organisation? Or is he working more than 24 hours a week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not registered with any careers service or anything like that. And he doesn't work full-time either - he's not working 24 hours a week or more.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Based on what you've told me, I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me summarise the situation:
- Alex is 17 years old
- He has left school and is not in any approved education or training
- He is not registered with a careers service, Connexions, or similar organisation
- He is not working 24 hours or more per week

Unfortunately, based on the eligibility rules, I need to share some important information with you.

For children aged 16 or over, you can normally only continue to receive Child Benefit if:
1. The child stays in **approved education or training**, OR
2. If a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for **20 weeks** if they register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services.

Since Alex is 17, has left education, and is **not** registered with a careers service or similar organisation, he would not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on my assessment, I'm sorry to say that **you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex** at this time.

**Reason:** For children aged 16 or over, Child Benefit can only continue if the child:
1. Remains in approved education or training (until they turn 20), OR
2. If they've left education/training at age 16 or 17, they register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services (which allows Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks)

Since Alex is 17, has left education, and is not registered with a careers service, he does not currently meet the eligibility criteria.

**However**, if Alex were to:
- Return to approved education or training, OR
- Register with a government-sponsored careers service

...you may then become eligible. If his circumstances change, you could look into claiming at that point.

I hope this helps clarify things. Is there anything else you'd like to know?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old. For children aged 16 or over, Child Benefit eligibility requires the child to either: (1) remain in approved education or training until they turn 20, OR (2) if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training, they must register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services (which allows Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks). Alex has left school, is not in any approved education or training, and is not registered with a careers service or similar organisation. Therefore, he does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The family has one child, Alex, aged 17. Since Alex is over 16, has left education, and has not registered with a careers service or similar organisation, he does not qualify for Child Benefit. The claimant is not eligible to receive Child Benefit for Alex under current circumstances.

Alex has left school, is not in any approved education or training, and is not registered with a careers service or similar organisation. Therefore, he does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

The claimant is not eligible to receive Child Benefit for Alex under current circumstances.
